page.title: Medicare Enrollment Periods Explained — IEP, GEP, SEP, AEP | Vernal Medicare
page.heading: Medicare Enrollment Periods Explained
page.description: Every Medicare enrollment window in one place: Initial (IEP), General (GEP), Special (SEP), and Annual (AEP) enrollment — when each applies, who it is for, and how to avoid penalties. Local Utah help.
page.summary: Medicare has several enrollment windows, and using the wrong one can cost you. Your Initial Enrollment Period (IEP) is the 7 months around turning 65. Miss it and you generally wait for the General Enrollment Period (Jan 1–Mar 31) — unless a life event gives you a Special Enrollment Period. Current members change plans during the Annual Enrollment Period (Oct 15–Dec 7).

faqs.question: Which enrollment period do I use?
faqs.answer: If you're new to Medicare, use your Initial Enrollment Period (the 7 months around your 65th-birthday month). If you missed it, you'll usually use the General Enrollment Period (January 1–March 31) unless a life event qualifies you for a Special Enrollment Period. Already on Medicare and want to change plans? That's the Annual Enrollment Period, October 15–December 7.

faqs.question: What happens if I enroll late?
faqs.answer: Enrolling in Part B or Part D after your Initial Enrollment Period (without a Special Enrollment Period) can trigger a permanent late-enrollment penalty added to your premium. See our page on missed enrollment and penalties.

sources.title: When does Medicare coverage start / enrollment periods

nextSteps.when: If you're turning 65 in the next few months,
nextSteps.action: your Initial Enrollment Period is the one to watch — call us and we'll map your exact dates so you never touch a penalty.

meta.ogDescription: IEP, GEP, SEP, and AEP — when each applies, who it is for, and how to avoid penalties.

body.introHtml: Medicare's alphabet of enrollment windows — IEP, GEP, SEP, AEP, OEP — trips a lot of people up, and using the wrong one (or missing one) can mean gaps in coverage or permanent penalties. Here's each window at a glance, with a link to the full details.
body.h2Table: The Enrollment Windows at a Glance

periods.name: General Enrollment Period (GEP)
periods.who: Missed your IEP and have no SEP

periods.name: Special Enrollment Period (SEP)
periods.who: Lost employer coverage, moved, lost Medicaid, etc.

periods.name: Annual Enrollment Period (AEP)
periods.who: Current members changing plans for next year

body.h2New: New to Medicare: Your Initial Enrollment Period
body.newHtml: When you first become eligible (usually at 65), your Initial Enrollment Period is a 7-month window — the 3 months before your birthday month, the month itself, and the 3 months after. This is the cleanest time to enroll, with no penalties. For the exact timing and how it interacts with employer coverage, see when to enroll in Medicare in Utah.
body.missedHtml: If you missed your IEP and don't qualify for a Special Enrollment Period, you'll generally enroll during the General Enrollment Period (January 1–March 31) — and a late-enrollment penalty may apply. A qualifying life event, though, may open a Special Enrollment Period with no penalty.
body.changingHtml: If you already have Medicare and want to switch plans, that's the Annual Enrollment Period (October 15–December 7), with a Medicare Advantage Open Enrollment Period (January 1–March 31) for one additional switch.
